output:
Below is a line-by-line review of your federal return, an explanation of the key tax rules, the errors we found, and the steps you’ll need to take—both to correct this year’s return and to avoid pitfalls next year.

1. Applicable Federal & State Tax Rules  
A. Reporting Self-Employment Income  
 • All 1099-NEC income (in your case $4,200) must be reported on Schedule C.  
 • Net profit from Schedule C flows to Schedule 1, line 3, of Form 1040.  
 • You then compute self-employment tax on Schedule SE (15.3% of 92.35% of net profit) and carry 50% of that SE tax to Schedule 1, line 15 as an “above-the-line” deduction.  

B. Deductibility of Mortgage Interest  
 • Mortgage interest on your primary residence (reported on Form 1098) is deductible on Schedule A if you itemize.  
 • You compare the total of your itemized deductions to the standard deduction for your filing status—whichever is greater.  

C. Who Qualifies as a Dependent  
 • A “qualifying child” must be under age 19 at year-end (or under age 24 if a full-time student), live with you more than half the year, and not provide over half of his own support.  
 • There is no gross-income test for a qualifying child; your 22-year-old college student with $18,000 of earnings can still qualify provided you furnish over 50% of his support.  

D. Correct Standard Deduction  
 • Filing Single for 2023: $13,850  
 • Filing Head of Household for 2023: $20,800  
 • You may file Head of Household instead of Single because you have a qualifying child.  

2. Review of Your Forms & Error Checklist  
 We have reconstructed your 2023 Form 1040 as follows (all numbers rounded to the dollar):

 A. Income  
  – W-2 wages (Form 1040 line 1): $68,500  
  – Schedule C net profit (line 8): $4,200  ← you had mistakenly entered $2,400 here  
  TOTAL INCOME (line 9): $72,700  

 B. Adjustments (Schedule 1)  
  – 50% of self-employment tax (Schedule SE calculates SE tax at $593; 50% = $297) on Schedule 1 line 15  
  ADJUSTED GROSS INCOME (AGI, Form 1040 line 11):  
    $72,700 – $297 = $72,403  

 C. Deductions  
  – You paid $9,800 of mortgage interest, but because your standard deduction as Head of Household ($20,800) exceeds that and any other itemizable expenses, you will elect the standard deduction.  
  – STANDARD DEDUCTION (Form 1040 line 12): $20,800  

 D. Taxable Income  
  TAXABLE INCOME (Form 1040 line 15):  
    $72,403 – $20,800 = $51,603  

 E. Tax Computation  
  – Federal income tax (per 2023 Head-of-Household tax tables):  
     • 10% on first $14,650 = $1,465  
     • 12% on next $36,953 = $4,434  
     TOTAL regular income tax = $5,899  
  – Self-employment tax (Schedule 2): $593  
  TOTAL FEDERAL TAX LIABILITY = $5,899 + $593 = $6,492  

 F. State Tax  
  – Most states begin with your federal AGI ($72,403) but then add or subtract items (e.g., some do not allow the SE deduction, some limit mortgage interest).  
  – Your state form’s computed liability didn’t match the AGI because you mis-entered your AGI (using $70,000 or so) and because you selected Single instead of Head of Household, which changes exemptions/deductions at the state level.  

3. Guidance for Correct Form Completion  
 A. Forms & Schedules You Need  
  1. Schedule C to report 100% of your $4,200 1099-NEC income (and any expenses, if you have them).  
  2. Schedule SE to compute self-employment tax and the 50% deduction.  
  3. Schedule 1 (Form 1040) to carry the SE tax deduction to AGI.  
  4. Form 1040—change your filing status to Head of Household.  
  5. Skip Schedule A (itemized deductions) since standard HOH deduction is larger.  
  6. Schedule 2 to add your self-employment tax.  
  7. Your state form—update AGI and filing status, and re-compute state deductions/exemptions in line with your state’s conformity rules.  

 B. Where to Enter Each Item  
  – Form 1040, line 1: $68,500 (W-2 wages)  
  – Form 1040, line 8: $4,200 (Schedule 1, line 3)  
  – Form 1040, line 10a: $297 (50% of SE tax)  
  – Form 1040, line 11: AGI $72,403  
  – Form 1040, line 12: Standard deduction $20,800 (HOH)  
  – Form 1040, line 15: Taxable income $51,603  
  – Form 1040, line 16: Tax from table $5,899  
  – Form 1040, line 23: Total tax $6,492 (includes Schedule 2 self-employment tax)  

4. Answers to Your Questions & Preventative Advice  
 A. Can You Legally Claim the Dependent?  
  Yes—your 22-year-old full-time student qualifies as a dependent if you provided over half of his support. Be prepared to document tuition payments, room & board, etc., vs. his earnings.  

 B. Why Do Federal & State Figures Differ?  
  – Filing status differences (Single vs. Head of Household) change brackets and deductions.  
  – State conformity—some states do not allow the 50% SE tax deduction or cap mortgage interest.  

 C. Avoiding Issues Next Year  
  – Quarterly estimated tax payments on your self-employment income (Form 1040-ES) to cover both income and SE tax.  
  – Keep a simple ledger or software for all 1099 income and related expenses.  
  – Save 1098 mortgage interest statements and track support provided to dependents.  
  – Review your filing status—Head of Household can save you $1,000s if you qualify.  

D. Recordkeeping Recommendations  
  – Maintain a folder (physical or digital) for 1099s, W-2s, 1098, tuition statements, and receipts for all business-related expenses.  
  – Reconcile quarterly to avoid surprises at year-end.  

By making these corrections—reporting the full $4,200 of self-employment income, claiming Head of Household status, taking the appropriate standard deduction, and filing the required schedules—you’ll arrive at an AGI of $72,403, taxable income of $51,603, and a total federal liability of approximately $6,492. Then update your state return to match your corrected AGI and HOH status. Please let me know if you need any template worksheets or a final review before filing!
